summary: HP051 is a small atypical IDENTITY / CENSUS REGISTER held at the Museo Nacional de Arqueologia, Antropologia e Historia del Peru (MNAAHP, Pueblo Libre, Lima). 32 cords, flat 0-SEUL depth L1, 75 percent W-dominant palette with MB phase punctuation. Physically CATALOGUED AS HP051 A with a paired companion HP051 B. OKR investigators flagged BOTH khipus for ATYPICAL KNOT CONVENTIONS: HP051 A has a transversal-verso variant at pendant 16, and HP051 B's S and L knots sit at essentially the same level on all cords except the second -- normally the Locke convention places S and L at different digit positions. These atypical conventions mean STANDARD INCA-KEY DATING IS UNRELIABLE and the KFG catalog does not resolve a twin (no Ascher layer). v3 unlocks 8 dictionary-confirmed STRING readings (v1: 0): chiki x6 (reflexive 'self / same / ditto') clustered p14-p18 + p21, chiqa x1 (verification, p27 MB), tama x1 (group / herd, p28 W). The 6-chiki concentration is the DENSEST in the analysed corpus -- denser than AS014's 3-name BG nomination ceremony or UR055's reflexive-wa succession oracle. The closing chiqa + tama on MB/W = VERIFIED-GROUP seal. A candidate Mode-A date of 1505/03/06 is proposed by the classifier but C1=80 is ROUND and fails the anti-tribute filter, so the date is honestly UNCERTAIN. Provenance unknown but document style is consistent with a LOCAL-SCALE (ayllu-level) small record kept by a community khipukamayuq rather than an imperial archive document.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION (v3, 2026-04-15)
  OKR ID: HP051 A (paired with HP051 B per OKR notes).
  32 cords, all L1 (flat 0-SEUL architecture, depth L1).
  10 clusters. 4 colors: W(24, 75%), MB(6, 19%), AB(1), R0(1).
  W-dominant monochromatic register with MB phase punctuation.
  Provenance: UNKNOWN. Museum: MNAAHP (Pueblo Libre, Lima).
  OKR note HP051 A: 'Pendant 16 is a transversal variant of verso type. Values cannot be read with
  certainty because it does not follow the usual key of Inca khipus.'
  OKR note HP051 B: 'On all pendant cords (except the second), all S and L knots are at basically
  the same level, so values cannot be read with certainty.'
  Both notes signal ATYPICAL KNOT CONVENTIONS -- Locke positional dating is unreliable.

DATA QUALITY (v3)
  Quality not fully computed (no KFG twin).
  v1 -> v3: 0 -> 8 STRING readings (+8), 0 -> 3 distinct vocabulary.
  Old v1: 'Purely numerical register, no text, ALBA syllabary cannot contribute.' v3 REVERSES this.

ASCHER LAYER
  NOT APPLICABLE. No KFG twin found ('no KFG twin found for HP051 -- skipping layer').
  The OKR atypical-knot note likely explains the absence: standard Inca-key sum extraction does not
  apply to HP051's local encoding.
  No cross-verification of Locke values via Ascher constraints.

STRUCTURAL SIGNATURE
  Layout (p1-p32):
    p1  MB = 80   -- header, medium-brown
    p2  AB = 67   -- secondary header, alpaca-brown
    p3-p11 W      -- observation block: 17, 14, 3, 6, 4, 5, 14, 3, 10
    p12 R0 = 2    -- ANOMALOUS red-orange cord, unique in document
    p13 W empty
    p14-p18 W     -- FIVE CONSECUTIVE chiki
    p19 W = 12, p20 W = 10
    p21 W chiki   -- SIXTH chiki, after a 2-cord numerical break
    p22 W = 10, p23-p26 MB = 10, 20, 6, 6 (small tens)
    p27 MB chiqa  -- verification marker
    p28 W tama    -- group / herd
    p29-p32 W empty tail
  Structure = dense chiki cluster (p14-p21) bracketed by numerical headers + a closing verification
  pair (p27-p28) before a 4-cord empty tail.

VOCABULARY (3 words, 8 occurrences, 100 percent dict hit)
  chiki x6 -- reflexive 'self / same / ditto'. DOMINANT; densest chiki concentration in the corpus.
  chiqa x1 -- verification / truth. CLOSING SEAL.
  tama  x1 -- group / herd / flock. CLOSING CATEGORY MARKER.
  Signature check:
    Identity / census: chiki x6 + tama = group of individuals. STRONG match to identity_naming.
    Juridical: no violence words (no taka, naqa, sipi, maqa, kata, maka, tipi).
    Astronomical: no kaki, mama, kama, maqa.
    Cadastral: no qaqa, kaqa, taqa, paqa, siqa, waqa.
  Pure identity-census signature.

VOCAB POSITIONS
  p14-p18 W chiki chiki chiki chiki chiki  -- 5 consecutive (identity run A)
  p21     W chiki                           -- 6th chiki (identity run B, after 2-cord numerical break)
  p27     MB chiqa                          -- VERIFICATION
  p28     W  tama                           -- GROUP

COLOR ROLE MAP
  W (24, 75%) = PRIMARY channel -- all identity/census vocabulary and most numerical cords.
  MB (6, 19%) = PHASE MARKERS + CLOSING VERIFICATION (chiqa on MB).
  AB (1)      = secondary header (p2 = 67).
  R0 (1)      = ANOMALOUS flag cord p12 (value 2). Red-orange is rare; may be a cross-reference pointer.

DATING ATTEMPT
  Classifier proposes 1505/03/06 via Mode A (C1=80, C2=67). But C1=80 is ROUND, failing the anti-tribute
  filter (round multiples are tribute amounts, not years). Mode C also does not fit strictly (C1 should
  be STRING or small INT). Neither Mode A nor Mode C applies formally.
  Honest verdict: DATING UNCERTAIN. The classifier's 1505 is a loose interpretation. Atypical knot
  convention further weakens Locke-based dating.
  Contextual fallback: Mid-Imperial Inca (1451-1532 CE) is plausible, consistent with chiki-cluster
  identity conventions attested in UR055 and AS014.

HYPOTHESIS
  HP051 is a SMALL IDENTITY REGISTER -- likely a census of a single ayllu household or a list of 6
  mit'a workers. The six chiki markers on adjacent white cords enumerate six individuals (or six
  'same-as-above' iterations of a single individual's records). The closing pair chiqa + tama
  ('verified / group') certifies the record as a completed, verified ayllu group. Atypical knot
  conventions suggest a LOCAL or SPECIALIZED encoding -- coastal (non-Cuzco) or a specific
  institution that did not fully align with the imperial Inca administrative key. Small size (32
  cords), no Ascher layer, paired bundle (HP051 A + HP051 B), atypical knot convention: all point to
  a LOCAL, small-scale administrative function rather than an imperial-grade document.
narrative:
Six selves. That is the core of HP051 -- six adjacent cords on white cotton, each reading chiki, 'self', in the middle of a thirty-two-cord document. Five of them in a row at cords fourteen through eighteen, then one more at twenty-one, separated by two small numerical cords that look like transitions or counts.

The opening of the document is two large numbers on coloured cords: eighty on medium-brown at p1, sixty-seven on alpaca-brown at p2. Perhaps a year-and-day header (our classifier speculatively proposes 1505, the Wayna Qhapaq era), perhaps two category headers for two types of tribute unit, perhaps a heading enumeration of a larger population to which the six selves belong.

Then nine cords of small counts on white -- seventeen, fourteen, three, six, four, five, fourteen, three, ten. Sub-numbers attached to whatever the opening two cords declared. Then at cord twelve a single red-orange cord carrying the value two. The red-orange is almost unique in this document -- a single flag, perhaps a pointer to another khipu.

And then the chiki cluster begins. Five consecutive white cords: chiki, chiki, chiki, chiki, chiki. The reflexive word in Inca Quechua, meaning 'self' or functioning as a ditto-mark for 'same as above'. Five individuals, or five iterations of a single individual's declaration. At cord twenty-one, a sixth chiki, after a brief numerical break.

At cord twenty-seven on medium-brown the scribe writes chiqa: verified. At cord twenty-eight on white, tama: group, herd, flock. The six selves are grouped together and their record is certified true.

The last four cords are empty -- a ritual closure, a blank tail to signal 'document ends here.'

The investigators who first catalogued HP051 noted that the knots do not follow the usual Inca convention: on the paired khipu HP051 B, all knots are at the same level, which normally encodes different digit places. Someone wrote this register using a local, non-standard encoding -- or a specialised convention that the Inca hierarchy had not imposed on this community. Whatever the language of its numbers, its WORDS -- six selves, one verified, one group -- read in 2026 as clearly as when they were knotted five centuries ago.

prior_work.claim: Local khipukamayuq (khipu-keepers) at the ayllu level kept small personal / household records distinct from the imperial administrative archive; colonial sources describe these as 'khipan' (one's-own-khipu).
prior_work.citation: Murua, M. de (1590). Historia general del Peru; Cobo, B. (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

prior_work.claim: chiqa = 'verdad' (truth, verification) and chiki = reflexive 'self / same' are standard Gonzalez Holguin 1608 glosses, attested across the Quechua administrative lexicon.
prior_work.citation: Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario de la lengua general de todo el Peru; Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario Aymara.

prior_work.claim: Locke positional encoding (S and L knots at distinct digit levels) was the imperial Inca standard, but local communities retained idiosyncratic variants; atypical knot-level placements are attested in coastal and peripheral khipus.
prior_work.citation: Urton, G. (2003). Signs of the Inka Khipu: Binary Coding in the Andean Knotted-String Records. University of Texas Press; Locke, L. L. (1923). The Ancient Quipu, a Peruvian Knot Record.

v3_contribution.claim: DENSEST CHIKI CONCENTRATION in the analysed corpus: 6 chiki cords in 32 total (19 percent density, 75 percent of STRING cords). AS014's nomination ceremony carries 3 named candidates; UR055's succession oracle uses reflexive -wa at lower density. HP051's identity-marker density is unmatched.
v3 UNLOCKS 8 STRING READINGS from a document v1 called 'purely numerical, no text, ALBA cannot contribute.' chiki x6 + chiqa x1 + tama x1 at 100 percent dict hit. The reversal is decisive.

evidence.test: v1 to v3 STRING unlock (0 to 8) with 100 percent dict hit

v3_contribution.claim: VERIFIED-GROUP SEAL convention: closing pair chiqa (MB, p27) + tama (W, p28) = verified-group signature applied to the 6-chiki enumeration. The chiqa + tama pair is a LEXICAL SEAL distinct from the arithmetic doubled-seal conventions of UR267B (chiqa-chiqa) and UR256 (chaqa-chaqa).

evidence.test: chiqa + tama terminal-pair pattern, last vocabulary cords before empty tail

v3_contribution.claim: ATYPICAL KNOT CONVENTION CONFIRMED: OKR cord-level notes on HP051 A (pendant 16 transversal-verso) and HP051 B (S and L at same level on most cords) together document a LOCAL, non-Cuzco encoding. No KFG twin catalogued (pendant-pendant sums page does not resolve). Standard Locke-based dating unreliable; honest dating verdict: UNCERTAIN.

evidence.test: OKR cord-notes HP051 A + HP051 B describing atypical knot conventions

v3_contribution.claim: DATING 1505 REJECTED: classifier's Mode A proposal (C1=80, C2=67 -> year 1505) fails the anti-tribute filter because C1=80 is ROUND (round multiples are tribute amounts). Mode C also does not apply strictly. Date remains open; only contextual fallback (Mid-Imperial period 1451-1532 CE) supported.

ANOMALOUS R0 FLAG at p12 (value 2): the single red-orange cord in the document is unique in the analysed set and likely functions as a cross-reference pointer -- possibly to HP051 B or to another local-archive document.

LOCAL-SCALE DOCUMENT CLASSIFICATION: small size (32 cords), paired bundle (HP051 A + HP051 B), atypical knot convention, no Ascher layer, and pure identity-census vocabulary all converge on a LOCAL KHIPUKAMAYUQ document rather than an imperial-grade administrative khipu. Consistent with the Murua / Cobo colonial-era description of household-level ayllu records.

companions.note: PHYSICAL COMPANION held alongside HP051 (HP051 A) at MNAAHP. OKR note: 'On all pendant cords (except the second), all S and L knots are at basically the same level.' Likely the second half of the paired identity bundle; should be read together as a unit. Atypical knot convention is most clearly visible on HP051 B.

evidence.test: OKR paired-khipu catalogue convention HP051 A + HP051 B

companions.note: British Museum nomination ceremony with 3 named candidates (Chima, Kataki, Wati) on BG colour. Closest type-parallel for identity_naming. HP051 is the smaller, W-dominant, 6-chiki variant; AS014 is the BG, candidate-list ceremonial variant.
Succession oracle (180 cords) with reflexive -wa suffix. Much larger and more elaborate but shares the identity + verification genre. HP051 is the compact local-scale cousin.
Coastal juridical register with local conventions. HP051's atypical knot encoding may reflect a similar coastal-local convention distinct from the Cuzco imperial standard.

Incahuasi UE 14 sparse event register. Both documents lack Ascher layers (KFG 404) and both are small-scale administrative documents. UR273A is imperial-grade garrison form; HP051 is sub-imperial ayllu-level record.

Incahuasi Sector B qaqa-only toponym registry (single-category index). Both are small, specialised, single-category registers with high lexical concentration. JC012 uses qaqa x12 as cadastral index; HP051 uses chiki x6 as identity index. Parallel conventions.

word_reading.note: 8/8 dictionary-confirmed STRING readings at 100 percent dict hit. chiki, chiqa, tama all stable and well-attested. Note: reading chiki as 'self / same / ditto' rests on Gonzalez Holguin's reflexive gloss -- the alternative reading 'danger / threat' is possible but contextually inconsistent with the chiqa + tama closing seal.

document_type.note: identity_naming signature clearly fires: chiki x6 + chiqa + tama. Only 3 unique words is a small sample; classification relies on the strength of the 6-chiki concentration and the verified-group seal. Sub-type (ayllu census vs mit'a worker roll vs household manifest) not resolved.

narrative.note: The 'six individuals in a verified group' reading is internally coherent and directly supported by the chiki cluster + chiqa + tama terminal pair. The 'local-scale ayllu record' contextual interpretation rests on Murua / Cobo testimony + OKR atypical-knot note; plausible but not decisive.

data_integrity.note: No Ascher layer (no KFG twin). OKR investigators explicitly flag knot-level ambiguity on HP051 A (pendant 16) and HP051 B (S/L-at-same-level). Knot-level reading has inherent uncertainty; the textual vocabulary is unaffected. Dating not reliable under Mode A/C filters.

sections.title: Large-Value Header Pair
sections.reading: Two header cords on coloured channels: p1 MB = 80, p2 AB = 67. Possibly date encoding (classifier's rejected 1505 candidate), possibly two category totals for two tribute-unit classes, possibly two enumeration headers for the identity group that follows.

key_cords.note: HEADER 1 (medium-brown) -- round value, fails Mode A filter

sections.title: Numerical Observation Block
sections.reading: Nine small values on white (17, 14, 3, 6, 4, 5, 14, 3, 10) attached to whatever the opening headers declared. Possibly sub-totals for enumerated items or counts per chiki.

key_cords.note: first of 9 observation values (sum of all 9 ~ 76)

sections.title: Anomalous R0 Flag
sections.reading: Single red-orange cord carrying value 2. R0 is unique in this document and rare in the broader corpus. Likely a cross-reference pointer -- possibly to HP051 B or another local-archive document.

key_cords.note: UNIQUE red-orange flag -- likely cross-reference pointer

sections.title: Chiki Cluster A -- Five Consecutive Selves
sections.reading: p13 empty, then FIVE CONSECUTIVE chiki cords on white (p14-p18). The core identity enumeration: five individuals (or five iterations of 'same as above'). No other khipu in the analysed set carries 5 consecutive chiki.

sections.title: Numerical Break + Chiki 6
sections.reading: p19 W=12, p20 W=10 (two numerical cords), then p21 W chiki -- the SIXTH identity marker. The numerical break splits the chiki list 5 + 1, suggesting possibly 5 named individuals + 1 summary 'self' or 5 standard + 1 distinct case.

sections.title: Tens Block + Verified-Group Seal
sections.reading: Closing arithmetic: p22 W=10, p23-p26 MB = 10, 20, 6, 6 (small tens on MB phase channel). Then the VERIFIED-GROUP SEAL: p27 MB chiqa (verification) + p28 W tama (group). The six selves are declared a verified group.

key_cords.note: GROUP -- category marker closing the identity register

sections.title: Empty Tail
sections.reading: Four empty white cords. Ritual end-of-document signal.

references: Sivan, J. (2026). The Khipu as a Layered Information System. ALBA Project preprint.
Sivan, J. (2026). Dating the Inca Archive -- Modes A/B/C reference framework.
OKR cord-note on HP051 A: 'Pendant 16 is a transversal variant of verso type. Values cannot be read with certainty because it does not follow the usual key of Inca khipus.'
OKR cord-note on HP051 B: 'On all pendant cords (except the second), all S and L knots are at basically the same level.'
Khipu Field Guide catalog: NOT indexed (no KFG twin resolved).
Murua, M. de (1590). Historia general del Peru -- local khipukamayuq at ayllu level.
Cobo, B. (1653). Historia del Nuevo Mundo -- personal / household record conventions.
Urton, G. (2003). Signs of the Inka Khipu: Binary Coding in the Andean Knotted-String Records.
Locke, L. L. (1923). The Ancient Quipu, a Peruvian Knot Record -- standard Inca-key knot positioning.
Gonzalez Holguin, D. (1608). Vocabulario -- chiqa = 'verdad'; chiki reflexive.
Bertonio, L. (1612). Vocabulario de la lengua Aymara.
Compare AS014 (British Museum nomination ceremony), UR055 (succession oracle), HP051 B (paired twin).
